text: Heap Pattern: Min-heap basics. Complete binary tree where parent <= children. Root is always minimum. Python: heapq module provides min-heap. heapq.heappush(h, val), heapq.heappop(h) removes and returns min. heapq.heapify(list) builds heap in O(n). For max-heap: negate values. Time: push O(log n), pop O(log n), peek O(1), build O(n).

Heap Pattern: Top K smallest/largest elements. For K largest: min-heap of size K. Push each element; when size > K, pop minimum. Result is K largest elements. For K smallest: max-heap (negate) of size K. Time: O(n log k). Compared to sorting O(n log n), heap is better when k << n. Alternative: QuickSelect for O(n) average but doesn't give sorted order.

Heap Pattern: K-way merge. Merge K sorted arrays or linked lists. Initialize min-heap with first element of each list (val, list_idx, element_idx). Pop min, add to result, push next element from same list. Time: O(n log k) where n=total elements, k=number of lists. Space: O(k). Use for: merge k sorted lists, external sort, kth smallest from k sorted arrays.

Heap Pattern: Find median from data stream. Maintain two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half. Balance so sizes differ by at most 1. Median = max of lower half (odd total) or average of both tops (even total). Insert: push to appropriate heap, rebalance if needed. Time: O(log n) per insert, O(1) for median. Classic 'running median' pattern.

Heap Pattern: Dijkstra's shortest path. Min-heap of (distance, node). Initialize with (0, source). Pop min distance node. If already processed, skip. Relax edges: for each neighbor, push (dist[node]+weight, neighbor) if it improves distance. Time: O((V+E) log V). Space: O(V+E). Use for: network delay, cheapest flights, shortest path in weighted graph.

Heap Pattern: Task scheduler / CPU scheduling. Process tasks in order of priority. Use max-heap (negate priority). For problems with cooldown: use time-based heap. Pop task with highest priority, execute, re-push with next available time. Template for tasks with cooldown n: pop from heap, push (next_available_time, count-1, task). Time: O(t log k) where t=total_time, k=distinct_tasks.

Heap Pattern: Reorganize string with heap. Place characters to avoid adjacent repetitions. Max-heap by frequency. Each step: pop most frequent char, place it; pop second most if previous is current (can't repeat). Push back with decremented count. If at any point only one char remains with count > 1 and it's same as last: impossible. Time: O(n log k) where k=distinct chars.

Heap Pattern: Minimum cost to connect ropes. Always merge two shortest ropes. Total cost = sum of all merge costs. Use min-heap: pop two smallest, push sum, add sum to total cost. Time: O(n log n). Same as Huffman coding tree construction. Greedy works because merging smallest ropes minimizes repeated additions (shorter ropes merged first contribute less to total cost).

Heap Pattern: Sliding window maximum with heap. Window of size k slides; find max in each window. Max-heap of (value, index). When popping, check if index is within current window; if not, skip (lazy deletion). Push new element. Top of heap (after removing stale) is window max. Time: O(n log n). Alternative: monotonic deque is O(n).

Heap Pattern: Meeting rooms and event processing. Schedule events with start/end times. Sort by start time. Min-heap of end times of active meetings. For each new meeting: if heap top (earliest ending meeting) <= current start, pop (room freed). Push current end time. Heap size = rooms in use. Max heap size = minimum rooms needed. Time: O(n log n).

Heap Pattern: IPO — maximize capital. Given projects with capital requirements and profits, pick at most k projects to maximize total capital. Locked projects: (capital, profit) sorted by capital. Available projects: max-heap of profits. Sweep: for each of k rounds, unlock all affordable projects (push to max-heap), pick most profitable (pop from max-heap). Time: O(n log n).

Heap Pattern: Kth largest in stream. Maintain min-heap of size k. On each insert: push to heap. If size > k: pop min. Heap top = kth largest so far. Space: O(k). This maintains exactly the k largest elements seen so far. Works for static arrays (O(n log k)) and online streams (O(log k) per element). Classic interview question.

Heap Pattern: Ugly numbers. Generate nth number whose only prime factors are 2, 3, 5. Min-heap starting with 1. Pop min, multiply by 2, 3, 5, push if not seen before. Use set to avoid duplicates. Pop n times. Time: O(n log n). Optimization: three pointers approach (DP) is O(n) without heap overhead.

Heap Pattern: Frequency sort / top K frequent elements. Count frequencies with Counter. Build max-heap of (freq, element) or use heapq.nlargest(k, count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]). For exactly K frequent: partial sort with heap is O(n log k) vs O(n log n) full sort. For all: bucket sort by frequency is O(n).

Heap Pattern: Furthest building you can reach. Use bricks or ladders to climb. Greedy with min-heap of ladder uses. When climbing height h: initially use ladder (push h to heap). When out of ladders: if h > smallest ladder use (heap top), swap: replace heap top with bricks, push h. If can't afford with bricks either: can't proceed. Time: O(n log L) where L=number of ladders.

Heap Pattern: Lazy deletion in heaps. When heap contains stale/invalid entries, mark them as deleted rather than removing immediately. On each pop, check if entry is valid; skip if not. Use version counter or separate 'deleted' set. Useful when deletion is expensive or impossible directly. Used in: Dijkstra (push new distances, ignore outdated), sliding window heap.

Heap Pattern: Heap sort implementation. Build max-heap from array (heapify). Repeatedly extract max to end of array. Heapify unsorted portion. Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1) in-place. Not stable. Build phase O(n) using Floyd's algorithm (heapify from n//2-1 down to 0). Each heapify call: O(log n). Total extract phase: O(n log n).

Heap Pattern: Median maintenance with ordered statistics. When you need to efficiently find median after each insertion. Alternative to two-heap approach: order statistics tree (balanced BST augmented with sizes). Each node stores subtree size. O(log n) insert and O(log n) kth-element query. Python doesn't have built-in; use sortedcontainers.SortedList for O(log n) operations.

Heap Pattern: Priority queue for simulation. Simulate events in time order. Event-driven simulation: priority queue ordered by event time. Pop earliest event, process, add new events to queue. Use for: simulation, network packet scheduling, process scheduling. Template: heapq.heappush(pq, (time, event_data)). Process events until queue empty or time limit. Time: O(E log E) where E=events.

Heap Pattern: Huffman encoding / optimal prefix codes. Build optimal prefix-free code for data compression. Min-heap of (frequency, character). Pop two minimum-frequency nodes, create parent with sum frequency, push parent. Repeat until one node remains (root of Huffman tree). Assign 0/1 for left/right traversal. Minimum expected code length achieved. Time: O(n log n). Applications: DEFLATE, ZIP, PNG compression.
